ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for chocolate frappuccino; closest defensible match is 'coffee beverage' (FOODON_03301521). Chocolate and blended ice components are not captured in this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers coffee-based beverages broadly; no specific code exists for blended chocolate coffee drinks with whipped cream. This is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No defensible SNOMED CT code identified for chocolate frappuccino as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chocolate frappuccino is one medium cup of approximately 350 ml, equivalent to roughly 350 g, as commonly served at specialty coffee outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Chocolate Frappuccino — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chocolate Frappuccino (cr_id D08020002), confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record without modification.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk-based blended coffee beverages — analogous estimate based on chocolate milk and coffee beverage GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chocolate frappuccino exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 is estimated based on the GI of chocolate milk (GI ~34–43, University of Sydney GI Database) and blended milk-coffee beverages, which are moderated by milk protein and fat slowing glucose absorption. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 40 × carbohydrate_g per serving 29.0 g) / 100 = 11.6. Carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 8.25 × 3.5 = 28.875 g ≈ 29.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.5 g sugar per 100 g; added sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk regardless of low sodium content (29 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage as its high added sugar content (7.5 g/100 g) independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; request sugar-free syrup and omit whipped cream, or substitute with unsweetened iced coffee with low-fat milk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.5 g sugar per 100 g and near-zero dietary fibre (0.09 g/100 g); liquid sugar from chocolate syrup is rapidly absorbed causing acute postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage due to its high sugar content (7.5 g/100 g) and negligible fibre, which together cause rapid postprandial glycaemic spikes; a sugar-free version with no whipped cream or a plain unsweetened iced coffee with milk is a safer alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from whipped cream and whole milk (~3.5 g/100 g) combined with high added sugar (7.5 g/100 g) contributes to dyslipidaemia and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this beverage as the combination of saturated fat (~3.5 g/100 g) and high added sugar (7.5 g/100 g) adversely affects LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels; request a reduced-fat, sugar-free version or choose unsweetened black iced coffee.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (7.5 g/100 g) and saturated fat from whipped cream are pro-inflammatory; coffee polyphenol benefit is negated by these components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as its added sugar and saturated fat content promote systemic inflammation; unsweetened coffee with plain low-fat milk retains coffee polyphenol benefits without the pro-inflammatory components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chocolate Frappuccino is a blended iced coffee beverage made from brewed coffee, chocolate syrup, whole milk, and crushed ice, typically topped with whipped cream. It is a popular specialty coffee drink widely available at coffee chains and cafes globally, and is commonly consumed as a mid-morning or afternoon treat. The drink has a rich, sweet chocolate-coffee flavour with a creamy, thick, and smooth texture from the blended ice and whipped cream topping. Per 100 g, it provides 96 kcal, 2.39 g protein, 5.77 g fat (approximately 3.5 g saturated), 8.25 g carbohydrate, and 7.5 g sugar, with low dietary fibre (0.09 g) and moderate calcium (72 mg). The high sugar content (7.5 g/100 g) classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), making it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The saturated fat from whipped cream and whole milk further elevates cardiovascular risk. Despite a relatively low glycaemic index (~40, analogous to chocolate milk), the glycaemic load per standard 350 ml serving is approximately 11.6, which is clinically meaningful for glycaemic management. The beverage is vegetarian and halal-suitable but not vegan or dairy-free. It is ultra-processed and not recommended as a regular dietary choice for individuals with cardiometabolic conditions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chocolate frappuccino?
Is chocolate frappuccino high in sugar?
Is chocolate frappuccino suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of chocolate frappuccino?
Chocolate frappuccino vs mocha frappuccino calories
Can I drink frappuccino if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08020002) with a confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. Saturated fat estimated at ~3.5 g/100 g based on typical whipped cream and whole milk composition, consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference data for similar blended coffee beverages.
notes: Saturated fat (3.5 g/100 g) is estimated from typical whipped cream and whole milk composition as the HPB source does not explicitly report saturated fat; this value is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for comparable blended coffee beverages (USDA FDC ID 174878, Starbucks Frappuccino Coffee Drink). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item and are not available from a directly matching approved source entry. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on chocolate milk GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 34–43 for chocolate milk); no direct GI measurement for chocolate frappuccino exists in the approved GI database. The dietary_tags 'low_calorie' from the input has been retained in meal context; 'low_sodium' is technically accurate (29 mg/100 g) but is not clinically meaningful given the high sugar content. This food is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and triggers hard gates for diabetes and hypertension suitability.
